summary: Jeff Atwood recounts the origin story of DONKEY.BAS, a simple BASIC game co-authored by Bill Gates and Neil Konzen during the early days of the IBM PC. Gates and Konzen wrote the program at 4 AM in an overheated closet that IBM required be locked for security. The Macintosh team famously mocked the crude game, amazed that Microsoft's co-founder would take credit for such poor work. Atwood notes that while DONKEY.BAS is an awkward part of Gates' programming legacy, Gates has shown good humor about it. Microsoft later created a tongue-in-cheek 3D remake in VB.NET to showcase how far BASIC had evolved over 20 years, though that version now suffers from compatibility issues with outdated DirectX dependencies.
key_points: Bill Gates was once an actual working programmer, with DONKEY.BAS serving as hard evidence of this era
The game was written at 4 AM in a locked, overheated closet because IBM required physical security for the prototype PC
The Macintosh team used DONKEY.BAS as an example of everything wrong with the IBM PC platform
Microsoft's VB.NET remake of Donkey demonstrated the evolution of BASIC over 20 years but now fails due to DirectX 8 dependencies
Various BASIC implementations have never been known for stellar compatibility across versions
money_quotes: It's hard to imagine now, but in the early days of Microsoft, Bill Gates was an actual programmer.
IBM insisted that we had to have a lock on the door and we only had this closet that had a lock on it, so we had to do all our development in there and it was always over 100 degrees.
We were amazed that such a thoroughly bad game could be co-authored by Microsoft's co-founder, and that he would actually want to take credit for it in the comments.
It's funny to think that DONKEY.BAS is part of Gates' legacy as a programmer.
It's a fairly short program, but it's also a painful reminder of how awkward programming was in 1981.
I suppose that's another enduring lesson of DONKEY.BAS; the various BASIC implementations have never been known for their stellar compatibility.
key_insight: Even the most successful tech moguls have embarrassing early code in their past, and the primitive state of early personal computing produced software that looks absurd by modern standards.
